subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : Frenkel defects are vacancy as well as interstitial defects.
Statement II : Frenkel defect leads to colour in ionic solids due to presence of F-centres.

Choose the most appropriate answer for the statements from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : Frenkel defects are vacancy as well as interstitial defects.
Statement II : Frenkel defect leads to colour in ionic solids due to presence of F-centres.

Choose the most appropriate answer for the statements from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution: A Frenkel defect is another form of a point defect which is created when an atom or cation leaves its original place in the lattice structure to create a vacancy while occupying another interstitial position within the solid crystal. Frenkel defect is dislocation defect, it is not interstitial defect, and it is not responsible for colour of solid.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a face centered cubic lattice atoms A are at the corner points and atoms B at the face centered points. If atom B is missing from one of the face centered points, the formula of the ionic compound is :
question: In a face centered cubic lattice atoms A are at the corner points and atoms B at the face centered points. If atom B is missing from one of the face centered points, the formula of the ionic compound is :
solution:
A atoms at corners  = 8 × 1 8 =  A (one atom)

B atom is missing at One face centre then will present at 5 faces

At face centre atom contribute 1/2 part

Number of B atom at Face centre = 5× 1 2 (B atom)

So formula of compound = A2B5

q: Atoms of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>hcp</mi></math> lattice and those of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> occupy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> of its tetrahedral voids. The percentage of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> in the lattice is (Nearest integer)
question: Atoms of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>hcp</mi></math> lattice and those of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> occupy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> of its tetrahedral voids. The percentage of element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> in the lattice is (Nearest integer)
solution:
Number of effective atoms in HCP lattice is =6

Number of atoms of X=6

Number of T.V. in h.c.p. =12 2×Number of atoms

Number of Y atoms =23×12=8 atoms.

% of X=614×100=42.86%

Round off to the nearest integer = 43

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of number of atoms present in a simple cubic, body centered cubic and face centered cubic structure are, respectively :
question: The ratio of number of atoms present in a simple cubic, body centered cubic and face centered cubic structure are, respectively :
solution:
ZSC = 8 × 18= 1 

[Atoms are only at the corners]

ZBCC = 8 × 18 +1= 1 + 1=2 

[Atoms are at the corners and at the body center]

ZFCC = 8 × 18 + 6 × 12= 1 + 3 = 4  

[Atoms are at the corners and at the face centers]

Here, Z= effective number of atoms in the unit cell.

Therefore, ZSC​: ZBCC​: ZFCC​=1 : 2 : 4

 




SC:BCC:FCC=1:2:4

subject: Chemistry
q: The radius of a calcium ion is $94 \mathrm{pm}$ and of the oxide ion is $146 \mathrm{pm}$. The possible crystal structure of calcium oxide will be
question: The radius of a calcium ion is $94 \mathrm{pm}$ and of the oxide ion is $146 \mathrm{pm}$. The possible crystal structure of calcium oxide will be
solution:
As per formula,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { radius ratio } & =\frac{\text { radius of cation }}{\text { radius of anion }} \\
& =\frac{94}{146}=0.643
\end{aligned}
$$
Since the value is between $0.414-0.732$ hence the coordination no. will be 6 and geometry will be octahedral.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a compound atoms of element $Y$ from ccp lattice and those of element $X$ occupy $2 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ of tetrahedral voids. The formula of the compound will be
question: In a compound atoms of element $Y$ from ccp lattice and those of element $X$ occupy $2 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ of tetrahedral voids. The formula of the compound will be
solution:
No. of atoms of $Y=4$
No. of atoms of $X=\frac{2}{3} \times 8$
Formula of compound will be $X_4 Y_3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
An atomic substance A of molar mass 12 g mol–1 has a cubic crystal structure with edge length of 300 pm. The no. of atoms present in one unit cell of A is

(Nearest integer)

Given the density of A is 3.0 g m m-1 and NAA=6.02×1023 mol-1
question:
An atomic substance A of molar mass 12 g mol–1 has a cubic crystal structure with edge length of 300 pm. The no. of atoms present in one unit cell of A is

(Nearest integer)

Given the density of A is 3.0 g m m-1 and NAA=6.02×1023 mol-1
solution:
The density of crystal can be calculated as follows,

ρ=Z×MNA×a3

ρ=density of the unit cell

Z=Effective atomic number

M=Molar mass

NA=Avogadro's number

a=Edge length of the unit cell

3=Z×126.02×1023×(3)3×10-24

Z=6.02×10-1×274=4

subject: Chemistry
q: All of the following share the same crystal structure except.
question: All of the following share the same crystal structure except.
solution:
$\mathrm{RbCl}, \mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{CsCl}$ share the same crystal structure except $\mathrm{LiCl}$.
$\mathrm{LiCl}$ is deliquescent. It crystalises as a hydrated LiCl. $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. Other alkali metal chlorides do not form hydrates.

subject: Chemistry
q: The appearance of colour in solid alkali metal halides is generally due to:
question: The appearance of colour in solid alkali metal halides is generally due to:
solution:
The appearance of colour in alkali metal halides is generally due to F-centres. F centers in $\mathrm{NaCl}$ produce a band of optical absorption towards the blue end of visible
spectrum, thus the colour appears yellow under transmitted light. On the other hand, $\mathrm{KCl}$ with $\mathrm{F}$-centers appears magenta, and $\mathrm{KBr}$ appears blue.

subject: Chemistry
q: An element with molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.7</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> forms a cubic unit cell with edge length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>405</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If its density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the radius of the element is approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> (to the nearest integer)
question: An element with molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.7</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> forms a cubic unit cell with edge length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>405</mn><mo> </mo><mi>pm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If its density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the radius of the element is approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> (to the nearest integer)
solution:
Density = Z×MNa×Volume

d=Z×276.02×1023×4.05×10-33

2.7=Z×276.02×1023×4.05×10-33

Z=4  ⇒fcc unit cell

For fcc 

unit cell  4r=2a

r=1.414×4054

=143.1675 pm

=143.17 pm

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following arrangements shows the schematic alignment of magnetic moments of antiferromagnetic substance?
question: Which of the following arrangements shows the schematic alignment of magnetic moments of antiferromagnetic substance?
solution: Substances which are expected to possess para-magnetic or ferromagnetism on the basis of unpaired electrons, but actually they possess zero net magnetic moment are called anti-ferromagnetic substance.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a face centred cubic lattice, atom A occupies the corner positions and atom B occupies the face centre positions. If one atom of $B$ is missing from one of the face centred points, the formula of the compound is
question: In a face centred cubic lattice, atom A occupies the corner positions and atom B occupies the face centre positions. If one atom of $B$ is missing from one of the face centred points, the formula of the compound is
solution:
Effective no.of $A$ atoms $=\frac{1}{8} \times 8=1$
Effective no.of $B$ atoms $=\frac{1}{2} \times 5$ (One is missing) $=\frac{5}{2}$
Therefore formula is $A_1 B_{\frac{5}{2}}=A_2 B_5$

q: <math> <mrow> <mtext>CsCl</mtext></mrow> </math> crystallises in body centred cubic lattice. If 'a' is its edge length then which of the following expressions is correct?
question: <math> <mrow> <mtext>CsCl</mtext></mrow> </math> crystallises in body centred cubic lattice. If 'a' is its edge length then which of the following expressions is correct?
solution:
8 chloride ions at 8 corners Cs + cation at body centre along body diagonal there will be one Cs + cation and 2  Cl − ions at ends of body diagonal.
2 r Cs + + 2 r Cl - = 3 a
a is edge length

q: An ionic compound has a unit cell consisting of A ions at the corners of a cube and B ions on the centres of the faces of the cube. The empirical formula for this compound would be
question: An ionic compound has a unit cell consisting of A ions at the corners of a cube and B ions on the centres of the faces of the cube. The empirical formula for this compound would be
solution:
$$
A=\frac{1}{8} \times 8=1
$$
(Corner)
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{2} \times 6=3$
(Face centre)
$\therefore \mathrm{AB}_3$

NA=6.0×1023 mol-1
question:

Given : Molar mass of Fe and O is 56 and 16 g mol-1 respectively.
NA=6.0×1023 mol-1
solution:
The density of the crystal can be calculated as follows,

d=Z×MN0×a3

d=densityZ=effective number of atomsM=Molar massN0=Avogadro's numbera=Edge length

4=Z×726×1023×125×10-24

Z=4.166≃4

q: An element crystallises in a face–centred cubic (fcc) unit cell with cell edge a. The distance between the centres of two nearest octahedral voids in the crystal lattice is:
question: An element crystallises in a face–centred cubic (fcc) unit cell with cell edge a. The distance between the centres of two nearest octahedral voids in the crystal lattice is:
solution:
In FCC octahedral voids are present at the edge centers and body center.

Minimum distance between centers of two octahedral voids

=x=a22+a22
=a24+a24=a2

q: The number of octahedral voids per lattice site in a lattice is_. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)
question: The number of octahedral voids per lattice site in a lattice is_. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)
solution:
If number of lattice points are N.

then effective octahedral voids =N

So, octahedral voids / lattice site =1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The radius of the largest sphere which fits properly at the centre of the edge of a body centred cubic unit cell is :

(Edge length is represented by 'a')
question:
The radius of the largest sphere which fits properly at the centre of the edge of a body centred cubic unit cell is :

(Edge length is represented by 'a')
solution:
For bcc

$$

\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}}{4}

$$

$$

\therefore \text { Empty space at edge }=a-2 R=a-\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{2}

$$

$=$ diameter of sphere.

$$

\therefore \quad \mathrm{r}_{\text {sphere }}=\frac{a-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{a}}{2}=\left(\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) \mathrm{a}=0.067 \mathrm{a}

$$

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound is formed by two elements X and Y. The element Y forms cubic close packed arrangement and those of element X occupy one third of the tetrahedral voids. What is the formula of the compound?
question: A compound is formed by two elements X and Y. The element Y forms cubic close packed arrangement and those of element X occupy one third of the tetrahedral voids. What is the formula of the compound?
solution:
Atoms of Element Y, are present in cubic close packing. In cubic close packing the number of atoms per unit cell is 4.

So, the effective number of element Y, per unit cell is 4

Element X, are present at one third of the tetrahedral voids.

Number of tetrahedral void =Twice the number of atoms in unit cell

Number of atoms of Element X are present unit cell =13×8=83

X : Y83 : 42   : 3

Formula of the compound =X2Y3

subject: Chemistry
q: The empirical formula for a compound with a cubic close packed arrangement of anions and with cations occupying all the octahedral sites in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Integer answer)
question: The empirical formula for a compound with a cubic close packed arrangement of anions and with cations occupying all the octahedral sites in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Integer answer)
solution:
In AxB
Effective number of B atoms =4 (in CCP)
Effective number of A atoms =4 (all O.V.)
So formula of the compound =A4B4=AB
So x=1

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following represents the lattice structure of A0.95O containing A2+, A3+ and O2– ions?

A.

B.

C.
question:
Which of the following represents the lattice structure of A0.95O containing A2+, A3+ and O2– ions?

A.

B.

C.
solution:
Applying the electrical neutrality principle in metal deficiency defect.

One A2+ is missing then two A2+ ions are converted into A3+ ion so 0.05 A2+ ion are missing and 0.05×2  A2+ ion are converted in A3+ ion so for each O2- ion total A2+ ion 0.85 and A3+ ion is 0.1.

Hence, A satisfies the condition.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The distance between Na+ and Cl- ions in solid NaCl of density 43.1 gcm-3 is .....×10-10 m. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1)
question:
The distance between Na+ and Cl- ions in solid NaCl of density 43.1 gcm-3 is .....×10-10 m. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1)
solution:
For NaCl Z=4 and M=58.5 gram

d=Z×MNA×Volume

43.1=4×58.56.02×1023×a3

a3=4×58.56.02×43.1×10-23

=0.9×10-23

=9×10-24

a=2.08×10-8 cm

dNa++Cl-=a2=2.08×10-102 m ≃1×10-10 m

subject: Chemistry
q: Na and Mg crystallize in BCC and FCC type crystals respectively, then the number of atoms of Na and Mg present in the unit cell of their respective crystal is
question: Na and Mg crystallize in BCC and FCC type crystals respectively, then the number of atoms of Na and Mg present in the unit cell of their respective crystal is
solution:
BCC - points are at corners and one in the centre of the unit cell
Number of atoms per unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}+1=2$
FCC - points are at the corners and also centre of the six faces of each cell
Number of atoms per unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}+6 \times \frac{1}{2}=4$

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of octahedral void (s) per atom present in a cubic close packed structure is :
question: The total number of octahedral void (s) per atom present in a cubic close packed structure is :
solution:
In CCP there are four atoms per unit cell octahedral voids are

12Edge centres14+1Body centre=4 O.V. [Contribution at edge centre is14 ]

Four octahedral voids are present in a unit cell i.e. four atoms.

Therefore, per atom one octahedral void will be present.

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the bcc unit cells of the solids 1 and 2 with the position of atoms as shown below. The radius of atom B  is twice that of atom A. The unit cell edge length is 50 % more in solid 2 than in 1 . What is the approximate packing efficiency in solid 2 ?
question: Consider the bcc unit cells of the solids 1 and 2 with the position of atoms as shown below. The radius of atom B  is twice that of atom A. The unit cell edge length is 50 % more in solid 2 than in 1 . What is the approximate packing efficiency in solid 2 ?
solution:
If radius of A is r radius of B will be 2r . Atoms in the body diagonal touch.

Therefore:
r+2r+2r+r=3 a

6 r=3 a or a=6r3

Packing Efficiency
=1×43πr3+1×43π(2r)3a3=43πr3×9(6r/3)3
=4×9×π×r3×333×216×r3=90.5 %

q: Glass is a
question: Glass is a
solution: Glass is a transparent or translucent super cooled liquid.

subject: Chemistry
q: Sodium metal crystallizes in a body centred cubic lattice with a unit cell edge of <math><mn>4.29</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math> . The radius of sodium atom is approximately:
question: Sodium metal crystallizes in a body centred cubic lattice with a unit cell edge of <math><mn>4.29</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math> . The radius of sodium atom is approximately:
solution:
For B.C.C lattice,

4 r = 3 a

where 'r' is radius of atom and 'a' is edge length of unit cell

r = 3 4 a = 1.732 4 × 4.29

= 1.86 Å

q: The parameters of the unit cell of a substance are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The crystal system of the substance is :
question: The parameters of the unit cell of a substance are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The crystal system of the substance is :
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>≠</mo><mi>β</mi></math> are parameters of monoclinic unit cell.

subject: Chemistry
q: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> crystallises in a body centred cubic unit cell with a cell edge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi>pm</mi></math>. The density of the element is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The number of atoms present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the element is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> crystallises in a body centred cubic unit cell with a cell edge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi>pm</mi></math>. The density of the element is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The number of atoms present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the element is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
M is body centred cubic, ∴ Z=2

Let mass of 1 atom of M is A

Edge length =300pm

Density =6 g/cm3

∴ 6 g/cm3=Z×A300×10-103=2×A27×10-24

A=81×10-24 g

Atoms of M=Total massMass of one atom = 18081×10-24 = 22.22×1023≅22×1023
